what: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: package, user can estimate steady-state BVAR(p) model Mattias Villani (Villani, 2009). estimation, user can produce forecasts (unconditional conditional) impulse response functions (orthogonalized generalized). goal use modern Bayesian tools (Stan) : ) estimate model specified original paper, ii) extend model different ways. Back day, extensions model seemed limited Mattias Villani time derive. See example, Clark (2011): “methodological sense, paper extends estimator Villani (2009) include stochastic volatility.” Later , writes: “(Special thanks due Mattias Villani providing formulas posterior means variances Π\Piand Ψ\Psi, generalize constant-variance formulas Villani 2009.)” recent example model mercy Professor Villani, can take look Technical Guide BEAR (Bayesian Estimation, Analysis Regression) toolbox ECB. page 122, Dieppe, Legrand, van Roye (2018) write: “Villani (2009) provides derivation case normal-diffuse prior distribution, incoming analysis restricted case.” Times different now, help Stan, can basically whatever can imagine. showcase , last section build steady-state BVAR model Clark (2011) [extension original model (Villani, 2009)], letting log volatilities follow correlated AR(1) processes instead uncorrelated driftless random walks. done without asking Professor Villani derivations. simply wrote model equations, put priors parameters, Stan rest!

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: can install development version SteadyStateBVAR GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("markjwbecker/SteadyStateBVAR")

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: steady-state BVAR(pp) model (Villani, 2009) yt=Ψdt+Π1(yt−1−Ψdt−1)+…+Πp(yt−p−Ψdt−p)+uty_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t yty_t kk-dimensional vector endogenous variables (time series) time tt, dtd_t qq-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time tt, (reduced-form) innovations ut∼Nk(0,Σu)u_t \sim N_k(0,\Sigma_u) independence time periods. Πℓ\Pi_\ell ℓ=1,…,p\ell=1,\dots,p (k×k)(k \times k) matrix, Ψ\Psi (k×q)(k \times q) matrix. Now E(yt)=μt=Ψdt\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t unconditional mean, steady state, process. Since long-horizon forecasts stationary VARs converge unconditional mean (steady state), naturally important forecasting perspective obtain precise inference Ψ\Psi. Note current version package allows dtd_t contain either constant, constant dummy variable, constant time trend. can stack (transposed) Πi\Pi_i matrices (kp×k)(kp \times k) matrix β\beta β=[Π1′⋮Πp′]\beta= \begin{bmatrix} \Pi'_1 \\ \vdots  \\ \Pi'_p \end{bmatrix} can rewrite model nonlinear regression (Karlsson, 2013) yt′=dt′Ψ′+[wt′−qt′(Ip⊗Ψ′)]β+ut′y_t' =d_t'\Psi' + \left[w_t'-q_t'(I_p \otimes \Psi') \right]\beta +u_t' wt′=(yt−1′,…,yt−p′)w_t'=(y_{t-1}',\dots,y_{t-p}') kpkp-dimensional vector lagged endogenous variables qt′=(dt−1′,…,dt−p′)q_t'=(d_{t-1}',\dots,d_{t-p}') qpqp-dimensional vector lagged deterministic (exogenous) variables, IpI_p (p×p)(p \times p) identity matrix ⊗\otimes denotes Kronecker product. likelihood written Stan code. goal estimate β,Ψ\beta, \Psi, Σu\Sigma_u, priors needed. First, prior independence β,Ψ\beta, \Psi Σu\Sigma_u assumed. Starting β\beta, use Minnesota prior vec(β)∼Nkpk[θβ,Ωβ]\mathrm{vec}(\beta) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kpk} \left[\theta_\beta,\Omega_\beta\right] prior means (elements θβ\theta_\beta) set E(Πℓ(,j))={κif ℓ=1andi=j0otherwiseκ={κlevelif variableiisinlevelκΔif variableiisindifference\begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)&= \begin{cases} \kappa & \text{} \ell = 1 \ \mathrm{} \ = j \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{cases}\\ \kappa&= \begin{cases} \kappa^{level} & \text{} \mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{level} \\ \kappa^{\Delta} & \text{}\mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{difference} \end{cases}\\ \end{aligned} , autoregressive coefficient Πℓ(,j)\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)} element (,j)\left(,j\right) Πℓ\Pi_{\ell} ℓ=1,…,p\ell=1,\dots,p. , Minnesota prior sets prior means elements β\beta 00, except elements relate first lags variables, set κ\kappa. variable ii level (e.g. nominal interest rate), κ=κlevel\kappa=\kappa^{level}, typical choices κlevel\kappa^{level} 11 0.90.9. Evaluating equations prior means, equation ii becomes random walk κlevel=1\kappa^{level}=1 persistent stationary AR(1) process κlevel=0.9\kappa^{level}=0.9. Since steady state exists process stationary, 0.90.9 recommended steady-state BVAR. variable ii difference (e.g. output growth), κ=κΔ\kappa=\kappa^{\Delta}, common choice κΔ\kappa^{\Delta} 00, .e. equation ii becomes (evaluating prior means) random walk expressed first differences. differenced variable still shows degree persistence (can examined ACF plot), suitable value κΔ\kappa^{\Delta} can (example) 0.60.6 instead 00. Moving prior variances, Ωβ\Omega_\beta diagonal matrix containing prior variances elements β\beta. specified Var(Πℓ(,j))={(λ1ℓλ3)2if =j(λ1λ2σiℓλ3σj)2if ≠j\mathrm{Var}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)= \begin{cases} \left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\ell^{\lambda_3}}\right)^2 & \text{} = j \\ \left(\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2\sigma_i}{\ell^{\lambda_3}\sigma_j}\right)^2& \text{} \neq j \end{cases} λ1\lambda_1, λ2\lambda_2, λ3\lambda_3 scalar hyperparameters known overall tightness, cross-equation tightness lag decay rate. Furthermore, σi2\sigma_i^2 (,)(,):th element Σu\Sigma_u, know therefore replace estimate. package, replaced least squares residual variance univariate autoregression variable ii pp lags (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable). Moving Ψ\Psi, prior use vec(Ψ)∼Nkq[θΨ,ΩΨ]\mathrm{vec}(\Psi) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kq}\left[\theta_\Psi,\Omega_\Psi\right] really core steady-state BVAR model. θΨ\theta_\Psi, specify prior beliefs location steady state, ΩΨ\Omega_\Psi, assume diagonal matrix, specify degree certainty prior beliefs. Finally, prior Σu\Sigma_u usual noninformative Jeffreys prior p(Σu)∝|Σu|−(k+1)/2p(\Sigma_u) \propto\left|\Sigma_u \right|^{-(k+1)/2} However, user wants, inverse Wishart prior can used instead Σu∼IW(V0,m0)\Sigma_u \sim \mathrm{IW}(V_0,m_0) V0V_0 scale matrix m0≥k+2m_0\geq k+2 number degrees freedom. uninformative prior can (package) specified setting V0=(m0−k−1)Σ̂uV_0=(m_0-k-1)\hat{\Sigma}_u Σ̂u\hat{\Sigma}_u least squares estimate VAR(pp) (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable), m0=k+2m_0=k+2. package also allows stochastic volatility (Random Walk AR1 specifications), let covariance matrix innovations vary time time-varying covariance matrix Σu,t\Sigma_{u,t} (details found respective vignettes).

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: estimate model Section 4.1 Villani (2009) perform impulse response analysis (un)conditional forecasting, simply run following code:
code: library(SteadyStateBVAR) data("Villani2009") yt <- Villani2009 yt <- ts(yt[1:102, ], start = start(yt), frequency = frequency(yt))  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bp <- which(time(yt) == 1992.75) dum_var <- c(rep(1,bp), rep(0,nrow(yt)-bp))  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj,                   p=4,                   deterministic = "constant_and_dummy",                   dummy = dum_var)  lambda_1 <- 0.2 lambda_2 <- 0.5 lambda_3 <- 1.0  #fol_pm = first own lag prior means fol_pm=c(0,   #delta y_f          0,   #pi_f          0.9, #i_f          0,   #delta y          0,   #pi          0.9, #i          0.9  #q          )  #psi_1 = Psi col 1 #psi_2 = Psi col 2  # default is 95% prior probability ('interval=0.95') theta_Psi <-    c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: i_f   ppi( 2.00,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y   ppi( 1.70,  2.30,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: pi   ppi( 4.00,  4.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: i   ppi( 3.85,  4.00,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean   #psi_2: q   )  Omega_Psi <-    diag(   c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i_f   ppi( 2.00,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y   ppi( 1.70,  2.30,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi   ppi( 4.00,  4.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i   ppi( 3.85,  4.00,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var    #psi_2: q   )   )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1,                    lambda_2,                    lambda_3,                    fol_pm,                    theta_Psi,                    Omega_Psi,                    Jeffrey=TRUE)  p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k kf <- 3 #foreign variables  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k)  for(i in 1:p){   rows <- ((i-1)*k + kf + 1) : (i*k)   cols <- 1:kf   restriction_matrix[rows, cols] <- 0 }  #block exogeneity for foreign variables bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)  bvar_obj$predict$H <- 12 bvar_obj$predict$d_pred <- cbind(rep(1, 12), 0)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 iter = 20000,                 warmup = 10000,                 chains = 4)  summary(bvar_obj)  #unconditional forecasts fcst <- forecast(bvar_obj,                  ci = 0.95,                  fcst_type = "mean",                  growth_rate_idx = c(4,5), #annual inflation/real GDP growth instead of QoQ                  plot_idx = c(4,5,6))  #conditional forecasts conditions <- data.frame(               var     = rep(6,12),               horizon = rep(1:12),               value   = seq(2, 8, length.out = 12))                cond_fcst <- conditional_forecast(bvar_obj,                     conditions,                     ci=0.68,                     fcst_type = "mean",                     plot_idx = c(4,6),                     growth_rate_idx = c(4)))  irf <- IRF(bvar_obj,            H=20,            response=5,#inflation            shock=6, #interest rate            type="median",            method="OIRF",            ci=0.68,            growth_rate_idx=5) #annual inflation instead of QoQ

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: Clark, T. E. (2011). Real-Time Density Forecasts Bayesian Vector Autoregressions Stochastic Volatility. Journal Business & Economic Statistics. 29(3), pp. 327–341. Dieppe, ., Legrand, R., van Roye, B. (2018). Bayesian Estimation, Analysis Regression (BEAR) Toolbox Technical guide. European Central Bank. Karlsson, S. (2013). Forecasting Bayesian Vector Autoregression. : Elliott, G. Timmerman, . (eds) Handbook Economic Forecasting. Elsevier B.V. Vol 2, Part B., pp. 791-897. Villani, M. (2009). Steady-state priors vector autoregressions. Journal Applied Econometrics. 24(4), pp. 630-650.

what: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
text: Computes plots impulse response functions (IRFs) fitted bvar object. Supports orthogonalised (OIRF) generalised (GIRF) impulse responses, optional conversion annual growth rates.

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
code: IRF(   x,   H = 16,   response = NULL,   shock = NULL,   type = c("median", "mean"),   method = c("OIRF", "GIRF"),   ci = 0.95,   t = NULL,   growth_rate_idx = NULL )

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
text: x bvar object passed fit. H Integer. forecast horizon IRF. Default 16. response Integer. Index response variable plot. NULL (default), responses plotted. shock Integer. Index shock variable plot. NULL (default), shocks plotted. type Character. Whether use "median" "mean" point estimate. Default "median". method Character. IRF method: "OIRF" orthogonalised "GIRF" generalised impulse responses. Default "OIRF". ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. t Integer. Time index covariance matrix using stochastic volatility models. NULL (default), last time period used. growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. NULL (default), conversion applied.

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
text: list three arrays: point estimate IRF, lower, upper credible bounds, dimension k x k x (H+1).

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model <- fit(model) irf <- IRF(model, H = 16, method = "OIRF") } # }

what: SteadyStateBVAR: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan — SteadyStateBVAR-package
title: SteadyStateBVAR: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan — SteadyStateBVAR-package
text: Provides estimation Bayesian Vector Autoregression ('BVAR') models steady-state priors via 'Stan'. estimation, package provides functions unconditional conditional forecasting, impulse response analysis. details steady-state BVAR model see Villani (2009) doi:10.1002/jae.1065 .

title: SteadyStateBVAR: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan — SteadyStateBVAR-package

code: Villani2009

title: Villani (2009) data — Villani2009
text: multivariate time series object 104 rows 7 columns: delta y_f foreign GDP growth pi_f foreign CPI inflation i_f foreign 3-month interest rate delta y domestic GDP growth pi domestic CPI inflation domestic 3-month interest rate q level real exchange rate defined q = s + p_f - p, p_f p foreign domestic CPI levels logs s log trade-weighted nominal exchange rate

what: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: Initialises steady-state bvar object. starting point models SteadyStateBVAR. creation, pass object sequentially setup, priors, fit build estimate model.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
code: bvar(data)

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: data numeric matrix time series data column variable row time period.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: object class bvar.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: model takes form $$y_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t,$$ \(y_t\) \(k\)-dimensional vector endogenous variables time \(t\), \(d_t\) \(q\)-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time \(t\). \(\Pi_\ell\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\) \((k \times k)\) matrix autoregressive parameters, \(\Psi\) \((k \times q)\) matrix steady-state parameters. Note \(\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t\) unconditional mean, steady state process. case homoscedastic steady-state BVAR, \(u_t \sim N_k(0,\Sigma_u)\), models stochastic volatility \(u_t \sim N_k(0,\Sigma_{u,t})\).

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: Villani, M. (2009). Steady-state priors vector autoregressions. Journal Applied Econometrics. 24(4), pp. 630–649.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)

what: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Computes plots conditional forecasts fitted bvar object. Conditions imposed specific variables specific horizons using method Waggoner Zha (1999). conditional unconditional forecasts plotted comparison.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
code: conditional_forecast(   bvar_obj,   conditions,   ci = 0.95,   fcst_type = c("mean", "median"),   growth_rate_idx = NULL,   plot_idx = NULL )

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: bvar_obj bvar object passed fit. conditions data frame three columns: var (integer index conditioned variable), horizon (integer forecast horizon condition imposed), value (numeric value variable conditioned ). ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. NULL (default), variables plotted levels. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: list three matrices: forecast, lower, upper, dimension H x k, well cond_draws, array posterior conditional forecast draws.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Waggoner, D. F. Zha, T. (1999). Conditional forecasts dynamic multivariate models. Review Economics Statistics, 81(4), 639-651.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Condition on variable 1 being 2.0 at horizon 4 conditions <- data.frame(var = 1, horizon = 4, value = 2.0) model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model <- fit(model) conditional_forecast(model, conditions = conditions) } # }

what: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: Estimates steady-state BVAR using  -U-Turn sampler (variant Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) via Stan. Uses data, setup, priors stored steady-state bvar object. Supports homoscedastic stochastic volatility (RW AR1) specifications.

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
code: fit(   x,   H = 1,   d_pred = NULL,   iter = 5000,   warmup = 2500,   chains = 2,   cores = NULL,   auto_write = NULL )

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: x steady-state bvar object passed setup priors. H Positive Integer. Forecast horizon. Default 1. d_pred Matrix size H x q. Future values deterministic variables d_t. iter Integer. Total number MCMC iterations per chain. Default 5000. warmup Integer. Number warmup (burn-) iterations per chain. Default 2500. chains Integer. Number MCMC chains. Default 2. cores Positive integer specifying number CPU cores used sampling. Must specified user. auto_write Logical indicating whether Stan models automatically written disk cache via rstan. Must specified user.

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: fitted steady-state bvar object : fit$stan: Stan fit object containing posterior draws fit$posterior_means: List posterior mean estimates: beta: k×k VAR coefficient matrix Psi: k×q steady-state parameter matrix Sigma_u: covariance matrix (k×k homoscedastic, T×k×k stochastic volatility) RW SV: , phi AR1 SV: , gamma_0, gamma_1, Phi fit$posterior_medians: List posterior median estimates: beta: k×k VAR coefficient matrix Psi: k×q steady-state parameter matrix Sigma_u: covariance matrix (k×k homoscedastic, T×k×k stochastic volatility) RW SV: , phi AR1 SV: , gamma_0, gamma_1, Phi

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: function selects appropriate Stan model based prior settings: Homoscedastic + Jeffreys prior: steady_state_bvar_homoscedastic_jeffreys_prior.stan Homoscedastic + inverse-Wishart prior: steady_state_bvar_homoscedastic_inverse_wishart_prior.stan Stochastic volatility (RW): steady_state_bvar_RW_stochastic_volatility.stan Stochastic volatility (AR(1)): steady_state_bvar_AR1_stochastic_volatility.stan stochastic volatility models, SV-specific priors priors$SV_priors merged Stan data list.

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2) bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt) bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p = 1) bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2)) bvar_obj$predict$H <- 1 bvar_obj$predict$d_pred <- matrix(1)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj, iter = 200, warmup = 50,                 chains = 1, cores = 1, auto_write = FALSE)  summary(bvar_obj) } # }

what: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: Computes plots forecasts fitted bvar object. Posterior predictive draws Stan used construct point forecasts credible intervals. Optionally converts level forecasts annual growth rates selected variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
code: forecast(   x,   ci = 0.95,   fcst_type = c("mean", "median"),   growth_rate_idx = NULL,   plot_idx = NULL,   show_all = FALSE )

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: x bvar object passed fit. ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. NULL (default), variables plotted levels. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted. show_all Logical. FALSE (default), last two years history shown alongside forecast. TRUE, full history shown.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: list three matrices: forecast, lower, upper, dimension H x k H forecast horizon k number variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model <- fit(model) fcst <- forecast(model, ci = 0.95, fcst_type = "mean") } # }

what: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: Calculates mean variance normal prior probability interval. Given lower upper bound (1-alpha) prior probability interval function recovers implied normal prior parameters. Useful specifying informative priors steady-state parameters (elements Psi).

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
code: ppi(l, u, interval = 0.95, annualized_growthrate = FALSE, freq = 4)

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: l Numeric. lower bound prior probability interval. u Numeric. upper bound prior probability interval. interval Numeric. prior probability mass within interval. Default 0.95, .e. 95%. annualized_growthrate Logical. TRUE, converts implied mean variance annualized scale quarterly/monthly etc. scale dividing freq. Default FALSE. freq Integer. data frequency (e.g. 4 quarterly). used annualized_growthrate = TRUE. Default 4.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: list two elements: mean var, giving mean variance implied normal distribution.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi

what: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: function prepares priors. Minnesota prior used autoregressive parameters, determined overall tightness, cross-equation tightness, lag decay rate. steady-state parameters, normal prior used. covariance matrix innovations, user can choose Jeffreys prior uninformative inverse-Wishart prior. Optionally enables stochastic volatility specification covariance matrix innovations (random walk AR(1)).

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
code: priors(   x,   lambda_1 = 0.2,   lambda_2 = 0.5,   lambda_3 = 1,   first_own_lag_prior_mean = NULL,   theta_Psi = NULL,   Omega_Psi = NULL,   Jeffrey = TRUE,   SV = FALSE,   SV_type = NULL,   SV_priors = NULL )

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: x steady-state bvar object passed setup. lambda_1 Numeric. Overall tightness Minnesota prior. Default 0.2. lambda_2 Numeric. Cross-equation tightness Minnesota prior. Default 0.5. lambda_3 Numeric. Lag decay rate Minnesota prior. Default 1. first_own_lag_prior_mean Numeric vector length k. Prior means first lag variable. NULL, defaults zero vector. theta_Psi Numeric vector. Prior mean vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters. NULL, defaults OLS estimates. Omega_Psi Numeric matrix. Prior covariance matrix vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters. NULL, defaults diagonal matrix variances 1000. Jeffrey Logical. TRUE, uses Jeffreys prior innovation covariance matrix. FALSE, uses uninformative inverse-Wishart prior. SV Logical. TRUE, enables stochastic volatility specification. Default FALSE. SV_type Character. Type stochastic volatility model. Must either "RW" "AR1". Required SV = TRUE. SV_priors List. User-supplied stochastic volatility priors. Required SV = TRUE.

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: steady-state bvar object appended priors list containing: theta_beta Prior mean vector vec(beta) constructed Minnesota prior Omega_beta Prior covariance matrix vec(beta) constructed Minnesota prior theta_Psi Prior mean vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters Omega_Psi Prior covariance matrix vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters Jeffrey Indicator Jeffreys prior usage Sigma_AR Residual variance estimates univariate AR fits, used Minnesota prior m_0 Inverse-Wishart prior degrees freedom (Jeffrey = FALSE) V_0 Inverse-Wishart prior scale matrix (Jeffrey = FALSE) SV Logical indicator stochastic volatility specification SV_type Stochastic volatility specification type SV_priors User-supplied SV prior list (SV = TRUE)

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))

what: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: Applies zero restrictions VAR coefficient matrix (beta) setting corresponding prior variances Omega_beta value near zero (prior means zero default Minnesota prior).

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: restrict_beta(x, restriction_matrix)

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: x steady-state bvar object passed priors. restriction_matrix numeric matrix dimension k*p x k entries 0 indicate coefficients restricted zero entries 1 indicate unrestricted coefficients.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: bvar object restriction matrix stored setup Omega_beta updated accordingly.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=2)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))                     p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k)  restriction_matrix[1, 1] <- 0 restriction_matrix[4, 2] <- 0  bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)

what: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
text: Prepares matrices needed prior specification estimation. Also computes OLS estimates.

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
code: setup(   x,   p = 1,   deterministic = c("constant", "constant_and_dummy", "constant_and_trend"),   dummy = NULL )

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
text: x steady-state bvar object created bvar. p Integer. lag order VAR. Default 1. deterministic Character. deterministic component include. One "constant" (default), "constant_and_dummy", "constant_and_trend". dummy Numeric vector dummy variable. used deterministic = "constant_and_dummy". Default NULL.

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
text: steady-state bvar object setup list containing matrices required prior specification estimation, also OLS estimates.

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj)

what: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: Produces time series plots posterior stochastic volatility estimates estimation sample together predictive paths forecast horizon fitted bvar object.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
code: stochastic_volatility_plot(   x,   ci = 0.95,   vol = "log_lambda",   plot_idx = NULL,   xlim = NULL,   ylim = NULL )

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: x bvar object estimated via fit stochastic volatility enabled. ci Numeric. Credible interval level. Default 0.95. vol Character. Volatility representation: "log_lambda" (default) "sd". plot_idx Integer vector. Variables plot. NULL, variables used. xlim Numeric vector length 2. Optional x-axis limits. ylim Numeric vector length 2. Optional y-axis limits.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: invisible list containing: estimated List posterior summaries estimation sample: mean: T × k matrix posterior means lower: T × k matrix lower credible bounds upper: T × k matrix upper credible bounds predicted List posterior summaries forecast horizon: mean: (T+H) × k matrix predictive means lower: (T+H) × k matrix lower credible bounds upper: (T+H) × k matrix upper credible bounds

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: function supports two volatility representations: "log_lambda": log-volatility states "sd": implied posterior standard deviations selected variable, function plots: posterior mean path (-sample) credible bands (-sample) predictive mean path (--sample) predictive credible bands (--sample) addition, posterior summary matrices estimation forecast periods returned invisibly.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model$SV <- TRUE model$SV_type <- "RW" model <- fit(model)  res <- stochastic_volatility_plot(model, ci = 0.95) str(res) } # }

what: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: Computes prints posterior summaries fitted bvar object. printed output depends whether model homoscedastic includes stochastic volatility (RW AR1 specification).

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
code: # S3 method for class 'bvar' summary(object, pars = NULL, stat = "mean", t = NULL, ...)

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: object bvar object passed fit. pars Character vector parameter names include. NULL (default), available parameters displayed. stat Character. Posterior summary statistic display: "mean" (default) "median". t Integer. Time index stochastic volatility covariance matrices. NULL, latest available period used. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: Returns input object invisibly.

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: Parameters printed structured blocks. Matrix-valued parameters displayed matrices, vector-valued stochastic volatility parameters (e.g. phi, gamma_0, gamma_1) printed named vectors. stochastic volatility models, time-varying covariance matrices Sigma_u,t can extracted specified time index. function supports standard VAR models stochastic volatility extensions. SV models, additional parameters may displayed depending specification: RW  SV: phi AR1 SV: gamma_0, gamma_1, Phi lik Output printed blocks manual formatting readability.

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2) bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt) bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p = 1) bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2)) bvar_obj$predict$H <- 1 bvar_obj$predict$d_pred <- matrix(1)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj, iter = 200, warmup = 50,                 chains = 1, cores = 1, auto_write = FALSE)  summary(bvar_obj) } # }

title: SteadyStateBVAR 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial development version. Preparing first CRAN submission.
